coredata.dc:title: A yellow carbon dots-based phosphor with high efficiency for white light-emitting devices
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Mengmeng
Xia, Chao
Xia, Jinfeng
Jiang, Danyu
Yu, Caiyan
Li, Huili
coredata.dc:description: The yellow-emitting carbon dots (CDs) were successfully synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal route using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as the starting material. The emssion peak of CDs shifted from 535 to 563 nm and the corresponding photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) enhanced from 10.4% to 23.1% when the reaction solvent of ethanol was replaced by deionized water and reaction temperature was increased by 20 °C. After CDs were effectively and uniformly attached to starch particles by hydrogen bonding and physical adsorption, a new type of environmentally friendly and yellow CDs-based phosphors has been developed. Effective dispersion of small CDs on the surface of large starch particles can lengthen the distance among CDs, as a result, the non-radiative decay process and photoluminescence quenching were steadily hindered. Highly efficient solid-state CDs phosphors with a PLQY of 66.9% were acquired. Combing with the excellent thermal stability and photostability, a daylight white LED based on a single color converter of yellow CDs phosphors was fabricated with a CRI of 83, luminous efficiency of 30.54 lm/W, and CIE coordinates of (0.3429, 0.2817). Suggesting that as-prepared CDs-based phosphors have a great potential in phosphor-based light-emitting devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solid-state phosphors
Starch
White LEDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of CDs phosphors-based white LEDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The PL properties of yellow-emitting CDs (a) and XRD patterns (b) of CDs, S/CDs-X phosphors, and pure starch.
The high resolution and Gaussian fitted XPS spectra of C 1s for CDs (a), S/CDs-50 phosphor (b) and pure starch (c), N 1s for CDs (d), S/CDs-50 phosphor (e) and pure starch (f).
The FTIR spectra of CDs, starch and S/CDs-X phosphors.

Excitation-wavelength-independent PL spectra of CDs (a) and S/CDs-50 phosphor (c) under different excitation wavelengths, emission and excitation (b) and UV–vis absorption spectra (d) of CDs, pure starch, and S/CDs-X phosphors.
The TEM image and size distribution histogram (inset) of CDs (a), fluorescence image of S/CDs-50 phosphor (b, λex = 450 nm), as well as SEM photos of pure starch (c) and S/CDs-50 phosphor (d).
The fluorescence intensity curve of S/CDs-50 phosphor heated to various temperatures in air atmosphere (a) and irradiating for different times by 455 nm lamp (b).
The EL spectra (a), photograph (inset of a) and CIE coordinates of as-fabricated white LED operated at 30 mA current.
Schematic diagram of formation process for CDs phosphors based on starch.
The PLQY, τ, KR, KNR and KR/ KNR of CDs, starch, and S/CDs-X phosphors.

head.ce:title: A yellow carbon dots-based phosphor with high efficiency for white light-emitting devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics and Advanced Instrument, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Materials Science, East China Normal University, Shanghai 200062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics and Advanced Instrument, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Materials Science, East China Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronics, Henan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Transparent Optofunctional Inorganic Materials, Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 200050, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Transparent Optofunctional Inorganic Materials, Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Jinming Avenue, Jinming District, Kaifeng, Henan Province, PR China.
Correspondence to: 3663 N. Zhongshan Rd., Shanghai, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The yellow-emitting carbon dots (CDs) were successfully synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal route using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as the starting material. The emssion peak of CDs shifted from 535 to 563 nm and the corresponding photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) enhanced from 10.4% to 23.1% when the reaction solvent of ethanol was replaced by deionized water and reaction temperature was increased by 20 °C. After CDs were effectively and uniformly attached to starch particles by hydrogen bonding and physical adsorption, a new type of environmentally friendly and yellow CDs-based phosphors has been developed. Effective dispersion of small CDs on the surface of large starch particles can lengthen the distance among CDs, as a result, the non-radiative decay process and photoluminescence quenching were steadily hindered. Highly efficient solid-state CDs phosphors with a PLQY of 66.9% were acquired. Combing with the excellent thermal stability and photostability, a daylight white LED based on a single color converter of yellow CDs phosphors was fabricated with a CRI of 83, luminous efficiency of 30.54 lm/W, and CIE coordinates of (0.3429, 0.2817). Suggesting that as-prepared CDs-based phosphors have a great potential in phosphor-based light-emitting devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of quasi-spherical-dimensional nanomaterials, are composed of internal carbon core with a sp2 hybrid nanocarbon structure and amorphous matrix containing rich functional groups and defects [1]. As a rising star of carbon family materials, CDs have attracted great attention in recent years. They have been widely used in biomedicine [2–4], ion detection [5–7], fluorescent ink [8,9], dual-mode imaging probe [10], nanosensors [11], optoelectronic devices [12], and so on, owing to excellent biocompatibility and water solubility, low toxicity, superior resistance to photobleaching, simple preparation, as well as easy surface functionalization [13–18].

White light-emitting diodes (white LEDs), as the fourth-generation lighting source, have rapidly become the most promising solid-state lighting devices of the 21st century. Recently, CDs have been investigated as potential solid-state fluorescent materials replacing conventional rare-earth-based phosphors for lighting and displays because of their tunable emission, low cost, and high photostability. It is found that CDs-based phosphors should be an ideal color conversion layer for application in white LEDs [19–23]. However, it is usually difficult for researchers to obtain solid-state CDs phosphors because the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching (AILQ) always accompanies when CDs in the solution are dried into films or powders. To overcome this drawback, a few studies about solid-state CDs phosphors based on physical or chemical adsorption have been reported [24–28]. For example, dispersing CDs in a NaCl and BaSO4 salt, or starch particles increases the distance among CDs to reduce or avoid AILQ of CDs themselves [25–27]. Additionally, adding steric hindrance chains such as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and silane coupling agents has been demonstrated to be an efficient strategy to acquire solid-state CDs-based phosphors [28,29]. However, the properties of these reported solid-state CDs-based phosphors are still unsatisfactory due to either the lower photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) or the shorter emission wavelength [29]. Therefore, it is still an urgent need to prepare environmentally friendly and highly luminescent solid-state CDs-based phosphors and promote CDs in white LEDs application.

In 2014, Qu's group found that starch was a good matrix and could integrate with CDs to get blue- and green-emitting starch/CDs phosphors with PLQY of 50% [30]. Combing them with blue GaN chips, a cool white light LED was fabricated with Commission Internationale de l′Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.26, 0.33). The other electroluminescent (EL) properties such as luminous efficacy, color rendering index (CRI), and the correlated color temperature (CCT) of white LED were not given there. It means that PLQY of starch/CDs phosphors need to be further enhanced and the emission spectrum should be tuned to the longer wavelength to meet the application requirements of daylight/warm white LEDs. Moreover, in order to avoid the reabsorption among multiphase phosphors and ensure the performance stability of device during manufacturing and operation, the blue chip combined with yellow-emitting phosphor should be a simplest and most efficient pattern.

Based on the above summary, in this paper, we first synthesized the yellow-emitting CDs using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as the starting material by a simple hydrothermal method. Different from the previous report [31], the reaction solvent of ethanol was replaced by deionized water and reaction temperature was increased from 180 °C to 200 °C. The emssion peak of the resultant CDs shifts from 535 to 563 nm and the corresponding PLQY enhances from 10.4% to 23.1%. Then, the yellow CDs was integrated with starch particles. The obtained solid-state CDs-based phosphors exhibit a PLQY as high as 66.9% which is much better than 50% reported by Qu [30]. Combing with the excellent thermal stability and photochemical stability, a daylight white LED based on a single color converter of yellow CDs phosphors was fabricated with a CRI of 83, luminous efficiency of 30.54 lm/W, and CIE coordinates of (0.3429, 0.2817). It suggests that as-prepared CDs-based phosphors have a great potentical in phosphor-based light-emitting devices.

o-PD and starch were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dialysis membrane was purchased from To-science Co., Ltd. The blue LED chips were obtained from Suzhou Nanojoin photoelectric Co., Ltd. Transparent silicone resin (YD65-5A, YD65-5B) used for white LED packaging was purchased from Raypower Optoelectronics (Suzhou) Co., Ltd. Deionized water was made by ourselves in laboratory. All chemicals are analytical reagents and used as received without any refining treatment.

1.2 g o-PD was firstly dissolved in 70 mL deionized water under magnetic stirring and then the solution was transferred into a 100 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave. After heating at 200 °C for 12 h in oven and cooling down to room temperature (RT) naturally, the claybank suspension was obtained. Subsequently, the agglomerated large nanoparticles were removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm microporous membrane and further dialyzed through a dialysis membrane with the MWCO of 500 Da for 48 h. Finally, a faint yellow CDs aqueous solution was obtained for further characterization.

1–30 g starch and 5 mL yellow CDs solution were placed in a beaker containing 30 mL deionized water under magnetic stirring for 6 h. The resultant mixing solution was centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and then the precipitate was dried at 70 °C for 2 h under vacuum to obtain solid-state CDs phosphors. Samples with different mass ratio of starch to CDs solution (10:1, 20:1, 50:1, 100:1, and 300:1) were named as S/CDs-X (X = 10, 20, 50, 100, 300), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CDs phosphors-based white LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.15 g S/CDs-50 phosphors, 0.5 g silicone resin (YD65-5A), and 0.5 g anhydride curing agent (YD65-5B) were homogeneously mixed by hand grinding. The obtained mixture slurry was directly coated onto blue chips (λpeak = 455 nm) by dipping and thermally cured in a vacuum oven at 150 °C for 1 h to form a yellow CDs-based luminescent layer. Finally, a white LED with high color rendering was fabricated, which is based on a single color converter of yellow CDs phosphors.

Ultraviolet/visible (UV/Vis) spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-3900) was adopted to measure the absorption spectra of the as-prepared CDs phosphors and CDs. PL spectra and PLQY were measured by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, FluoroMax-4) containing an integrating sphere unit (Horiba Jobin Yvon, F3029). The phase evolution and morphology were characterized by a M21XVHF2Z X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Mac Science Co., Ltd.), a Hitachi TM3000 scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM), respectively. Raman spectra were recorded on a Raman Spectrometer with 632.8 nm He-Ne laser as an irradiation source (RM-1000, Renishaw). The chemical environments, element compositions and functional groups of the samples were analysed by a multifunction imaging photoelectron spectromenter (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) and a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet, Magna-IR 750). A NUV/Vis/Near IR spectrophoto-colourimeter (PMS-80) was employed to investigate EL properties such as emission spectra, luminous efficiency, CRI, CCT, and CIE color coordinates of the as-fabricated White LEDs. All of the measurements were accomplished at RT. For the thermo-stability measurement, the yellow CDs phosphors were bedded in a sample cavity and heated to the desired temperature by a high-temperature fluorescence controller (Tianjin Orient KOJI Co., Ltd., TAP-02). Please refer to our previous reports for the detailed test process [32–34].

The yellow-emitting CDs was synthesized by using o-PD as the starting material through a simple hydrothermal method. Compared with the previous report [31], after the reaction solvent of ethanol was replaced by deionized water and reaction temperature was increased from 180 °C to 200 °C, the resultant CDs give the bright yellow emission instead of green. The emssion peak shifts from 535 to 563 nm and the corresponding PLQY enhances from 10.4% to 23.1%. Fig. 1a shows the detailed PL properties of CDs.

From the previous reports we know that the AILQ in solid-state CDs can be decreased or avoided by dispersing CDs in other solid matrix. So, CDs-based phosphors were prepared by using the starch as a matrix to increase the distance among CDs particles in this work. The formation process of CDs phosphors based on starch is shown in Scheme 1. Starch and yellow-emitting CDs solution were placed in deionized water by magnetic stirring. The resultant mixing solution was centrifugated to remove residual CDs, and the precipitate was dried under vacuum to obtain solid-state CDs phosphors. The mass ratio of starch to CDs solution was adjusted from 10:1, 20:1, 50:1, 100:1, to 300:1 to optimize PL properties of CDs-based phosphors, corresponding to S/CDs-X (X = 10, 20, 50, 100, 300), respectively. The phase compositions of CDs and S/CDs-X phosphors were identified by XRD, as presented in Fig. 1b. The broad diffraction peak at 19.5° of CDs indicates the poor crystallinity and amorphous structure of CDs. S/CDs-X phosphors integrating CDs with starch exhibit the same diffraction peaks at 5.9°, 15°, 17.3°, 19.7°, 22.5°, 24.3° and 34.4° as pure starch powders, corresponding to potato starch B-type crystals. While the diffraction peak of CDs at 19.5° is absent owing to the less proportion of CDs in S/CDs-X phosphors and a large overlap between the characteristic peaks of CDs at 19.5°and starch at 19.7°. It also indicates that the integrating of CDs with starch has no obvious effect on their respective crystal structure [35–37].

The Raman spectra were obtained to further investigate the chemical bond information of pure starch, CDs and S/CDs-X phosphors. The results are shown in Fig. S1. The Raman spectrum of CDs presents two characteristic vibration peaks located at 1376 cm−1 and 1520 cm−1, respectively, which are attributed to D band representing the lattice defects and disordered structure of graphite and G band coming from an inherent characteristic of natural graphite, relevant to stretching vibration of E2g in the sp2 hybrid surface of carbon atoms [38]. In other words, the prepared CDs have partially disordered graphitic structure. In the Raman spectrum of starch, vibrational peak at 2900 cm−1 originates from the symmetric and anti-symmetric stretching vibrations of C-H bonds, and 1500–800 cm−1 is generated by the vibration of glucose monomer. Peaks at 1340–1200 cm−1 are contributed to the stretching vibrations of C˭O and C˭C bonds, as well as the bending vibrations of C-OH and C-C-H. The vibration band at 1108 cm−1 pertains to C-O and C-OH bonds [39–42]. The vibrations at 800–400 cm−1 originate from skeleton of the glucose pyranose ring, where 475 cm−1 is the main characteristic peak of starch [40]. After CDs are integrated with starch, samples exhibit all the vibration bands from two kinds of components. Moreover, the characteristic peaks of starch get stronger and stronger with its content increasing.

As seen in Fig. S2, XPS was carried out to analyze the element compositions and prove the effective combination of CDs and starch. Clearly, CDs contain three peaks at 284.8 eV, 398.8 eV and 531.9 eV corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s [43], suggesting that as-prepared CDs are composed of C, N and O elements. The same diffraction peaks also exist in pure starch except for N 1s. However, when CDs are added to starch with different proportions, the peak of N 1s coming from CDs begins to appear and the intensity gradually enhances with the ratio of CDs rising. The calculated atomic percentage of N in the samples (Table S1) increases from 1.21% in S/CDs-300, 3.28% in S/CDs-10, to 17.41% in pure CDs. All these results prove that CDs have successfully combined with starch.

The high resolution XPS spectra of CDs, S/CDs-50 phosphor, and pure starch are illustrated in Fig. 2. It is obviously visible that the signal peak of C1s locates at 284.74 eV for S/CDs-50 phosphor (Fig. 2b), which is similar with 284.8 eV for CDs (Fig. 2a). After Gaussian fitting, five peaks are obtained for both CDs and S/CDs-50 phosphor, corresponding to C-C/C˭C, C-N, C-O, C˭N/C˭O, and O˭C-OH [44]. While for pure starch, the fitted peaks for C-N and C˭N are absent. Similarly, N 1s signal can also be fitted to three typical peaks by Gaussian for both CDs and S/CDs-50 phosphor, which match with three types of nitrides: pyridine N, amino N, and pyrrolic N [45], as seen in Fig. 2d and e. However, no characteristic peak of nitrogen is detected in the starch (Fig. 2f). Additionally, there are also a few differences for the signal peak of O1s in Fig. S3. Although three samples contain two signal peaks from C-O and C˭O, the peak integration area is various. The area of C˭O in CDs is greater than that of C-O, the starch is the opposite. While, two areas are basically identical in S/CDs-50 phosphor. All these results confirm that there exists the successful and efficient binding between CDs and starch.

The FTIR spectra in Fig. 3 further demonstrate that there are a large amount of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs, e.g. hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and amide groups. All of these functional groups have proven that CDs are of great hydrophily. Literature reports have confirmed that three FTIR characteristic peaks of starch are located at 3280 cm−1 (O-H), 1630 cm−1 (COOH beding vibration), and 926 cm−1 (C-O-H), respectively [46–48]. From Fig. 3, it can be observed that CDs have the similar vibration peaks except for 926 cm−1 (C-O-H). However, for S/CDs-X phosphors, all the characteristic absorption peaks are marked. Therefore, from both XPS and FTIR analyses, we can conclude the formation mechanism of CDs-based phosphors. CDs are effectively adsorbed on starch particles by both hydrogen bonding from surface functional groups and physical forces.

To explore the fluorescence properties of CDs and S/CDs-X phosphors, their PL and absorption spectra were measured and shown in Fig. 4. CDs display excitation-independence emission characteristic with the strongest emission peaks at 563 nm under 390 nm excitation (Fig. 4a). The corresponding PLQY reaches 23.1%. After integrating CDs with starch particles, the emission bands of S/CDs-X phosphors (Fig. 4b) exhibit a blue shift of 18 nm from 563 of CDs to 545 nm under the excitation wavelength of 410 nm irrespective of the starch content. Simultaneously, PL intensities of all samples are enhanced dramatically compared to that of CDs solution, and a maximum is reached for S/CDs-50. It similarly shows the excitation-independence emission position but contains the highest intensity at 410 nm (Fig. 4c). The PLQY improves to 66.9% from 23.1% of CDs solution. All these PL properties authenticate that the adsorption of CDs on starch particles can effectively increase the distance among CDs and as a result AILQ in the solid state is hindered. Excitedly, a new type of environmentally friendly and low-cost CDs-based phosphor is explored in this work.

As we know that the luminescence of CDs is different from the traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs). The luminescent mechanisms for CDs is still unclear up to know. Two popular PL models have been proposed for CDs: one is related to the band gap transitions in conjugated π-domains, which is ascribed to quantum size effects; and the other comes from surface defects on the CDs [8,49–52]. In our work, the excitation-independence emission characteristic for both CDs and S/CDs phosphors implies that surface states not particle size should be the dominant factor influencing the PL behavior. Moreover, the corresponding PL excitation curve of CDs in Fig. 1a also exhibits that there is only one peak with a position quite close to that of the absorption band in the lower energy region, confirming that as-prepared CDs own identical absorption structures and luminescent centers. These results are similar to those reported full color emission CDs by Xiong and Lin [49,53].

The UV–vis absorption spectra of yellow-emitting CDs, starch, and S/CDs-50 phosphor were recorded and are present in Fig. 4d. The CDs have a broad absorption even to visible region, including three peaks at 215, 232, and 289 nm in the ultraviolet region and a broad peak at 423 nm in the visible region. The UV absorption bands are attributed to the π-π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 domain, the visible absorption peaked at 423 nm is assigned to the n-π* transitions of C˭N/C˭O and/or C-N [18,49,54]. Pure starch has almost no any absorption in the visible region due to without C˭N and C-N bonds in its molecule structure, see XPS spectra in Figs. 2c and 2f. While, the S/CDs-50 phosphor has a stronger absorption from 400 to 650 nm than CDs solution, meaning the successful adsorption of CDs on the surface of starch particles. Moreover, starch neither competes with CDs to absorb excitation light nor reabsorbs emission from CDs, and as a result, the enhanced fluorescence is obtained in the S/CDs-X phosphors.

The TEM image in Fig. 5a shows that as-prepared yellow-emitting CDs own the spherical morphology and a uniform dispersion. The size distribution histogram (inset of Fig. 5a) discloses that the diameters of the CDs distribute in the range of 6.5–13.8 nm centered at 9.5 nm. It is broader than 1.5–4.5 nm and 4–8 nm reported by Xiong [49] and Lin [53], respectively. However, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of about 78 nm for emission spectra is analogous even narrower than their results. Therefore, it can be said that the broad PL emission for CDs in our work is not ascribed to the different sizes of dots. That is to say, PL behavior for CDs should be attributed to surface states not particle size. In order to confirm in further, the FWHM of CDs and S/CDs-X phosphors under different excitation wavelengths were also measured and compared in Tables S2 and S3. Obviously, no remarkable variation is observed.

Fig. 5b presents fluorescence image of S/CDs-50 phosphor. It can be seen that the S/CDs-50 phosphor with a broad size distribution of 2–60 µm emits the strong yellow fluorescence under photoexcitation of 450 nm. The emission comes from the whole starch particle surfaces, further confirming that CDs are successfully and uniformly adsorbed on starch surface. This uniform adsorption on the surface of matrix with larger particles effectively hinder AILQ in solid state. SEM photos shown in Fig. 5c-d indicate that the integration of CDs with starch hardly influences on the morphology and size distribution of pure starch because of the fairly small size of CDs.

The PLQY and time-resolved fluorescent spectra (τ) of CDs and S/CDs-X phosphors were recorded to deeply understand the mechanism of PL enhancement for solid-state CDs-based phosphors, and the PL decay curves are fitted with monoexponential decay kinetics and the lifetimes are presented in Table 1. Radiative (KR), nonradiative decay rates (KNR), and the ratio of KR/ KNR could be estimated by the following functions:
(1)
τ =
(
K
R
+
K
NR
)
− 1
(2)
PLQY =
K
R
(
K
R
+
K
NR
)
− 1
where KR and KNR are the radiative and non-radiative decay rates, τ is the average lifetime. All calculated values have been tabulated in Table 1. Clearly, the PLQY, τ, and KR significantly increase with the starch ratio increasing and reach the maxima for S/CDs-50 phosphor. At this time, KR is 13.57 × 107 s−1, 2.02 times higher than KNR. It indicates that CDs can uniformly disperse on the surfaces of starch particles. The lengthened distance among CDs effectively suppresses non-radiative decay processes and inhibits AILQ. So, the PLQY of S/CDs-50 phosphor can reach 66.9%, while only 23.1% for CDs solution.

Excellent thermal stability and photostability are essential for LEDs application because their working temperature can reach nearly 130 °C [34]. Hence the stability of S/CDs-50 phosphor for white LEDs packaging was explored as shown in Fig. 6. The fluorescence intensity curve (Fig. 6a) of S/CDs-50 phosphor heated in air to various temperatures illustrates that no the usual fluorescence attenuation is observed for S/CDs-50 phosphor with temperature rising from 30 °C to 180 °C. Instead, PL intensity is enhanced and at 180 °C, runs up to 163% of that at RT. Soon afterwards, although the fluorescence intensity begins to decrease with temperature, it is still higher than that at RT. Fig. 6b gives the changing curve of intensity for S/CDs-50 phosphor with time under blue lamp of 455 nm irradiation for 6 h. Almost no reduction in intensity suggests good photostability of the phosphor. Thus, the CDs-based phosphors should be an ideal material for white LEDs.

As seen in Fig. 7a, the white LED based on S/CDs-50 phosphor has a broad emission spectrum ranging from 420 to 700 nm, almost covering the entire visible area. The emission peak at 455 nm comes from the blue LED chip and 596 nm belongs to S/CDs-50 phosphor which exhibits a red shift of 51 nm after combining with silicone resin. The LED presents a bright white light with CRI and CCT of 83 and 4613 K, respectively, under forward current driving (the inset of Fig. 7a). The luminous efficacy is 30.54 lm/W, and the CIE coordinates locate at (0.3429, 0.2817) (Fig. 7b) which is very close to the pure white light of (0.33, 0.33). Of course, the EL properties of white LED can be regulated by varying the CDs content in starch/CD phosphors to meet some special requirements. It is concluded that the CDs-based solid-state phosphors are potential candidates for practical lighting applications.

In summary, we reported a universal method to obtain solid-state CDs phosphors with excellent properties via absorbing yellow CDs on the surface of pure starch particles. By varying the reaction solvent from ethanol to deionized water and increasing reaction temperature from 180 °C to 200 °C, The emssion peak of the resultant CDs shifts from 535 to 563 nm and the corresponding PLQY enhances from 10.4% to 23.1%. Then, the yellow CDs was integrated with starch particles. The obtained solid-state CDs-based phosphors exhibit a PLQY as high as 66.9% which is much better than 50% that in previous report. Combing with the excellent thermal stability and photostability, a daylight white LED based on a single color converter of yellow CDs phosphor was fabricated with a CRI of 83, luminous efficiency of 30.54 lm/W, and CIE coordinates of (0.3429, 0.2817). Suggesting that as-prepared CDs-based phosphors have a great potentical in phosphor-based light-emitting devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51472087) and Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Transparent Opto-functional Inorganic Materials, Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. KLTOIM201606).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.10.056.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence

One-step synthesis of orange luminescent carbon dots for Ag+ sensing and cell imaging

Carbon dots as temperature nanosensors in the physiological range
Synthesis of silica-based carbon dot/nanocrystal hybrids toward white LEDs
A facile, green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from oatmeal for cell imaging
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
